summary: I was born in Chiba Prefecture and lived there until I was 14 years old. My father was originally from there, and I obtained my non-immigrant passport from the Chiba Prefectural office when I came to America. I have returned to Japan several times and visited friends from my childhood neighborhood in Chiba. It holds many memories of my early life and family.
cues: non-immigrant passport from Chiba Prefectural office

summary: My father was the boss of a fruit ranch in Napa, California, and I came to the United States to join him when I was 14. During the 1906 San Francisco earthquake, many people from Chiba Prefecture fled to Napa and worked on my father's ranch. After working as a farm laborer there, I eventually became a foreman at the ranch. Napa was where I started my life in America and learned to work hard.
cues: father was boss of fruit ranch in Napa
1906 San Francisco earthquake refugees came to Napa
worked as farm laborer and foreman

summary: I heard that during the 1906 San Francisco earthquake, many people from Chiba Prefecture escaped from San Francisco and came to my father's ranch in Napa. My father provided them with food and work picking pears. This event affected the Japanese immigrant community and brought many people to Napa where my family was working.
cues: people from Chiba Prefecture escaped San Francisco

summary: San Francisco was the city affected by the 1906 earthquake, which caused many Japanese immigrants from Chiba Prefecture to flee. My father was in Napa at the time, and many of these refugees came to work on his fruit ranch. Although I did not live in San Francisco, the events there impacted my family and community.
cues: father in Napa at the time

summary: My sister Yasuko married a man from Hiroshima and has been living in Sacramento for a few years. She visits us regularly. Also, after my brother and I paid off our ranch in Oregon, he moved to Sacramento to grow strawberries, and I helped him there before buying his share.

My sister Yasuko married a man from Hiroshima, and they have been living in Sacramento for several years. She is the youngest daughter of my sister and visits us often. Hiroshima is connected to my family through her marriage.
cues: sister Yasuko married man from Hiroshima

summary: After selling the fruit ranch in Napa, my brother and I bought an Italian prune ranch in Salem, Oregon. We worked hard there and paid off half of the ranch each. However, work was scarce, so eventually we moved on to other places like Sacramento and Imperial Valley. Oregon was an important step in our farming career.
cues: bought Italian prune ranch in Salem, Oregon
worked hard and paid off ranch
moved on due to lack of work

summary: In Salem, Oregon, my brother and I bought an Italian prune ranch and worked hard to pay it off. It was a challenging but rewarding experience. After some time, my brother moved to Sacramento to grow strawberries, and I bought his share of the Oregon ranch.

I worked in the Imperial Valley where the temperature reached 115 degrees and the wind felt like fire. We grew cantaloupes by contract on a 40-acre ranch, working long hours sometimes until 10 p.m. The roads were unpaved and dusty, and transportation was by horseback. It was tough work, but I earned about $10 a day and persevered.

Many people smuggled themselves into America from Mexico during my time working in Imperial Valley. My brother and I were among the few who had passports, but others did not. I remember visiting a man named Nakamura who was caught and jailed in Brawley before being deported back to Mexico. It was a difficult situation for many young immigrants.

I visited a man named Nakamura in jail in Brawley, California, after he was caught smuggling himself into America from Mexico. He was only 27 and was sent back to Mexico as it was his first offense. This experience made me feel sorry for him and highlighted the struggles of immigrants at that time.

After working in Imperial Valley, my brother and I went to Arizona, specifically Phoenix, where it was very hot. We worked on Mr. Yamamoto's ranch, who was a graduate of Waseda University and managed 200 acres using horses. One night a big thunderstorm blew away our camp, but luckily no one was hurt. It was hard work but a memorable experience.

Mr. Yamamoto, the ranch owner in Arizona where I worked, was a graduate of Waseda University. He was a frank and hearty man who managed a large ranch using horses. Knowing his background made me respect him and the work we did there.
cues: Mr. Yamamoto graduated from Waseda University
worked on his ranch in Arizona

summary: Before going to Imperial Valley, I worked picking oranges in North Pomona with my brother. We stayed at Mr. Sasaki's camp until June. I preferred working silently in the fields, and it was better for my brother's health after he caught measles in Los Angeles. North Pomona was one of the places where we labored hard to make a living.

Immigrants had to get their passports from the Kanagawa Prefectural office, but I was a non-immigrant and received mine from the Chiba Prefectural office. This distinction was important when I came to America. It shows the different immigration procedures depending on status and origin.
cues: immigrants got passports from Kanagawa Prefecture
I got mine from Chiba Prefecture

summary: When I was about 25 years old, I attended Heald College for a while to further my education. However, I quit after some time, partly because of the Woodbridge Act passing in 1924. Education was important to me, but circumstances made me focus on farming and family.

The Woodbridge Act passed Congress when I was 27 years old in 1924, which prohibited Japanese from entering America after July of that year. This law affected my plans and the Japanese immigrant community. After it passed, I went back to Japan to meet a prospective bride but eventually married my present wife and returned to America in 1922.
cues: went back to Japan to meet bride
